enclosure_behavior.reason: PLA softens at 57°C — passive enclosure heats up with bed, causing heat creep and clogs

materials.name: PLA Matte

enclosure_behavior.reason: Same as PLA Basic — passive enclosure overheats with bed >45°C

notes: Matte texture naturally hides layer lines — fine layers (0.15 mm) make little visual difference vs standard.
Use wall loops 3+ — under-extrusion gaps are more visible on matte surfaces.

materials.name: PLA Silk

enclosure_behavior.reason: Same as PLA — keep door open to prevent heat creep

notes: Lower fan speed than standard PLA — more heat retained gives the characteristic sheen.
Needs +5-10°C nozzle vs standard PLA for proper flow — higher viscosity from metallic pigments.
Slow outer wall speed (40–60 mm/s) for best surface gloss.

materials.name: PLA-CF

enclosure_behavior.reason: PLA-CF is still PLA-based — same enclosure rules apply

warnings: Hardened steel nozzle required — carbon fibers will destroy brass/stainless nozzles within hours.
Do NOT use 0.2mm nozzle — carbon fibers cause immediate clogging. 0.4mm minimum, 0.6mm recommended.
notes: Produces stiff, dimensionally stable parts with a matte carbon-fiber texture.
Abrasive fibers will gradually wear AMS PTFE tubes — inspect periodically.

materials.name: PETG Basic

enclosure_behavior.reason: Low ambient temp reduces interlayer bonding strength for PETG

materials.build_plate_display: Textured PEI (add glue stick or release agent)
notes: PETG bonds aggressively to smooth PEI — always use textured PEI or apply a glue stick layer.
Too little cooling → stringing. Too much → poor layer adhesion. Start at 40% and adjust.

materials.name: PETG HF

enclosure_behavior.reason: PETG HF performs best with stable temperatures

warnings: Set Max Volumetric Speed to the value shown — Bambu Studio's default is too high for this material and causes stringing.
notes: Max Volumetric Speed for 0.4 mm nozzle: 16 mm³/s — Bambu Studio's default (24) is incorrect for this material.

materials.name: ABS

enclosure_behavior.reason: ABS warps severely without enclosure heat. Enclosed chamber holds 45–55°C for reliable layer adhesion.

warnings: ABS emits styrene VOCs during printing. Use enclosed printer with carbon filter. Ensure good room ventilation — do not print in unventilated spaces.
notes: Requires fully enclosed printer — open-frame printers will produce warped, failed prints.
Apply glue stick evenly to engineering plate before every print for reliable adhesion.
Print slowly (outer wall ≤ 60 mm/s) for best layer bonding and warp resistance.

materials.name: ASA

enclosure_behavior.reason: Like ABS, ASA warps severely without enclosure. Enclosed chamber ensures consistent temperatures.

warnings: ASA emits styrene VOCs — same as ABS. Enclosed printer with carbon filter required. Ensure good room ventilation.
notes: ASA is the outdoor-rated alternative to ABS — superior UV and weather resistance.
Apply glue stick to engineering plate before every print.
Functionally identical to ABS in terms of printing requirements.

materials.name: TPU 95A

enclosure_behavior.reason: TPU benefits from good cooling. Open frame preferred to prevent heat buildup.

warnings: TPU feeds poorly through AMS — use rear spool holder directly into extruder for best results.
Print slowly (max 60 mm/s) — TPU stretches during fast retraction causing under-extrusion and jams.
notes: Minimal retraction required (0.3 mm) — flexible filament stretches instead of retracting properly.
95A Shore hardness = semi-flexible. Stiffer than rubber bands, softer than rigid plastics.
Disable combing mode for best results — TPU oozes during travel moves.

materials.name: TPU 90A

enclosure_behavior.reason: TPU 90A benefits from good cooling. Open frame preferred — enclosed heat can cause heat creep in the cold zone with soft TPU.

materials.build_plate_display: Textured PEI (no glue) or Cool Plate / Smooth PEI (with glue)

drying.display: Required — 70 °C / 8h blast oven or 75–85 °C / 8h heatbed (wet TPU 90A = hissing, stringing, weak layers)

warnings: AMS and AMS Lite are not compatible — use the rear external spool holder and feed directly.
Dry before printing — moisture causes hissing, stringing, and weak layer adhesion.
Print at max 30 mm/s — faster speeds cause under-extrusion and extruder skipping.
notes: Zero retraction (0mm) is recommended — flexible filament stretches instead of retracting properly. Use 0.0–0.5mm maximum.
Disable Pressure Advance (PA = 0) — PA calibration does not work correctly with elastic filaments.
Disable flow dynamic calibration before printing — it produces unreliable results on flexible materials.
90A Shore = soft flexible rubber. Softer and more elastic than 95A but more printable than 85A.
For tight-fit parts, add 0.1–0.2mm extra tolerance vs rigid material designs — TPU relaxes slightly after printing.
Supports are difficult to remove from TPU parts — avoid if possible, or use PLA/PETG as support material.
Do not apply glue to textured PEI — the combination is excessively sticky and will tear soft parts during removal.
Enable Avoid Crossing Walls (combing) to reduce open-air travel moves and minimise stringing.
Cool Plate Supertack must NOT be used — adhesion is too strong and will tear soft TPU parts.
Spool must be elevated — filament discharge port must be higher than the toolhead loading port.
On Bambu P series: load through PTFE tube (push filament through manually first, then reconnect).
Manual unloading required after heating to 250°C — automatic unloading with cutter is not supported.
Place model in the middle-front of the heatbed to reduce filament friction through the PTFE tube.

materials.name: TPU 85A

enclosure_behavior.reason: TPU 85A requires maximum cooling for dimensional accuracy. Enclosed environments cause heat creep in the cold zone — keep open.

materials.build_plate_display: Textured PEI (no glue) or Cool Plate / Smooth PEI (with glue)

drying.display: Required — 70 °C / 8h blast oven or 75–85 °C / 8h heatbed. Wet 85A = hissing, spiderweb strings, print failure.

warnings: TPU 85A requires a 0.6mm or larger nozzle — 0.2mm and 0.4mm nozzles are not supported and will clog.
AMS and AMS Lite are not compatible — feed directly from the rear spool holder. Do not route through PTFE tube on X/P series.
Dry before every print — moisture causes popping, spiderweb stringing, and complete print failure. 65°C / 8h minimum.
Print at max 20 mm/s — faster speeds cause the soft filament to buckle in the extruder and jam.
notes: Zero retraction (0mm) is strongly recommended — any retraction stretches the soft filament and causes jams downstream.
Disable Pressure Advance (PA = 0) — elastic filament behaviour breaks PA calibration.
Disable flow dynamic calibration — it produces inaccurate results on flexible materials.
On Bambu printers: loosen the extruder idler tension screw before printing — default tension flattens 85A filament and jams it.
85A Shore = very soft rubber, like a shoe insole. Extremely flexible, requires slow speeds and careful setup.
For tight-fit parts, add 0.3–0.5mm extra tolerance vs rigid designs — 85A relaxes significantly after printing.
Supports in TPU fuse strongly with the part — use PLA as support material, or avoid supports by design.
Textured PEI can grip 85A too strongly — let the bed cool completely before removal.
Enable Avoid Crossing Walls (combing) to reduce stringing from open-air travel moves.
Printing live from a filament dryer box is best practice for 85A — it reabsorbs moisture quickly during long prints.
Cool Plate Supertack must NOT be used — adhesion is too strong and will tear soft TPU parts.
Spool must be elevated — filament discharge port must be higher than the toolhead loading port.
On Bambu P/X series: load filament from ABOVE the printer directly into the extruder — PTFE tube loading is NOT supported for 85A.
Manual unloading required — heat to 250°C, slowly press unload button and gently pull filament out. Auto-unload with cutter not supported.
Use a new or dedicated hotend for TPU — residue from other filaments can cause clogs in soft TPU.
Before loading: flush the hotend at 250°C with PLA Matte 3-5 times to clear any residue.

materials.name: PVA (Support)

enclosure_behavior.reason: PVA is PLA-compatible — same enclosure rules; low bed temp means little risk

warnings: PVA is a support-only material — pair with PLA as the primary material using AMS multi-material setup.
PVA dissolves in water (12–24h soak). Do not store in humid environments — degrades rapidly without sealed dry box.
notes: Water-soluble support material — produces perfectly clean support interfaces on complex geometries.
Only compatible with PLA as the primary model material (same bed temp range).
After printing: submerge in warm water (30–40°C) for 12–24h until supports dissolve completely.

materials.name: PETG-CF

enclosure_behavior.reason: PETG-CF benefits from stable ambient temperature to reduce warping on larger parts

warnings: Hardened steel nozzle required — carbon fibers will destroy brass/stainless nozzles within hours.
Do NOT use 0.2mm nozzle — carbon fibers cause immediate clogging. 0.4mm minimum.
notes: Carbon fiber fill adds stiffness and dimensional stability compared to standard PETG.
Parts are matte and slightly rough — excellent for functional enclosures and structural brackets.
PETG-CF bonds aggressively to smooth PEI — always use engineering plate or textured PEI.

materials.name: PA (Nylon)

enclosure_behavior.reason: PA warps significantly without stable ambient temperature — enclosure strongly recommended for anything larger than 50mm

warnings: PA (Nylon) is extremely hygroscopic — absorbs moisture from air within hours of opening. Dry before EVERY print and store in a sealed dry box with desiccant.
High shrinkage (1.5%) — use generous brim (8–12mm) and enclosure to prevent warping and layer separation.
notes: Outstanding toughness and fatigue resistance — ideal for functional hinges, gears, snap-fits.
Slight flexibility when thin — better impact resistance than rigid plastics like ABS or PLA.
Prints best with dry box feeding directly into printer — even 30 min of air exposure degrades quality.

materials.name: PC (Polycarbonate)

enclosure_behavior.reason: PC shrinks severely without a warm enclosed chamber — warping and delamination guaranteed on open printers

warnings: PC requires a fully enclosed printer — open-frame printers will produce warped, delaminated failures.
Bed temperature 110°C+: ensure your printer's bed can reach and sustain this temperature.
notes: Highest impact and heat resistance of all common FFF materials — used in bulletproof glass.
Optically transparent when printed with high infill — unique property among FFF materials.
Requires very slow outer wall speed (≤40 mm/s) for good layer adhesion at these temperatures.

materials.name: PA-CF (Nylon Carbon Fiber)

enclosure_behavior.reason: PA warps significantly without stable ambient temperature — enclosure strongly recommended for anything larger than 50mm

warnings: PA (Nylon) is extremely hygroscopic — absorbs moisture from air within hours of opening. Dry before EVERY print and store in a sealed dry box with desiccant.
High shrinkage (1.5%) — use generous brim (8–12mm) and enclosure to prevent warping and layer separation.
notes: Carbon fiber reinforced nylon — very strong but requires hardened nozzle

materials.name: PET-CF (PET Carbon Fiber)

enclosure_behavior.reason: PETG-CF benefits from stable ambient temperature to reduce warping on larger parts

materials.build_plate_display: Textured PEI or Engineering Plate (glue recommended)
warnings: Hardened steel nozzle required — carbon fibers will destroy brass/stainless nozzles within hours.
Do NOT use 0.2mm nozzle — carbon fibers cause immediate clogging. 0.4mm minimum.
notes: PET-CF offers higher heat resistance than PETG-CF with excellent dimensional stability

materials.name: HIPS

enclosure_behavior.reason: ABS warps severely without enclosure heat. Enclosed chamber holds 45–55°C for reliable layer adhesion.

materials.build_plate_display: High Temp Plate or Engineering Plate
notes: Commonly used as soluble support material for ABS — dissolves in D-Limonene
